name: Object Pronouns — lo, la, me, te

cards.front: What are the direct object pronouns in Spanish?
cards.back: me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las
cards.grammarNote: Direct object pronouns replace the noun that directly receives the action: 'Envío el informe' → 'Lo envío' (I send it). They go before the conjugated verb.

cards.front: Do you have it? (masc.) / Do you have it? (fem.)
cards.grammarNote: Lo replaces masculine nouns; la replaces feminine ones: ¿El informe? Lo tengo. ¿La reunión? La cancelé.

cards.front: What are the key indirect object pronouns?
cards.grammarNote: Indirect object pronouns show to whom or for whom the action is done: 'Te explico el proyecto' (I explain the project to you). They also go before the conjugated verb.

cards.front: I'll explain it to you now.
cards.grammarNote: When two pronouns appear together, the indirect comes first: te (indirect) + lo (direct) = te lo. This is one of the most common double-pronoun structures.

cards.front: She sends it to me by email.

cards.grammarNote: When le or les is followed by lo/la/los/las, the indirect pronoun changes to se: le + lo → se lo. This avoids the awkward 'le lo'. Se lo digo = I tell it to him/her.

cards.front: I'm going to call you. (two ways)
cards.back: Te voy a llamar. / Voy a llamarte.
cards.grammarNote: With infinitives, the pronoun can attach to the end or go before the conjugated verb. Both are equally correct.

cards.front: I'm sending them now. (feminine plural — e.g., las respuestas)
I'll explain the project to them.
